content.text: Microservice observability was hard. Agentic AI observability is harder. An agent can invoke 15 tools, call the LLM 8 times, branch across three parallel sub-agents, and silently produce a wrong answer — all within 30 seconds and without triggering a single conventional alert. Building unified observability means instrumenting every layer of the stack — model calls, tool invocations, reasoning steps, infrastructure metrics — and correlating them into a single coherent view that lets you understand not just what happened, but why the agent behaved the way it did.

Unified observability for agentic AI is the practice of collecting, correlating, and querying telemetry signals from every layer of an agent system — the LLM inference calls, tool executions, agent state transitions, orchestration logic, and underlying infrastructure — through a single integrated pipeline rather than isolated, siloed tools for each layer.

It builds on the three pillars of classic observability — logs, metrics, and traces — and extends them with agent-specific signals: reasoning chain spans that capture multi-step decision sequences, token usage attribution per agent step, tool call success/failure rates, and task-level outcome tracking. The defining characteristic of unified observability is that a single query can traverse from a user-visible agent failure down through the LLM span, the specific model call parameters, the tool invocations triggered, and the infrastructure load at that moment.

Agent failures are non-deterministic and multi-causal. A user complaint that "the agent gave the wrong answer" might trace back to any of: an LLM hallucination on step 3, a tool returning stale data on step 5, a timeout causing the agent to skip a verification step, a model temperature set too high producing creative but incorrect reasoning, or a prompt template bug introduced three days ago. Without unified observability, you have no systematic way to determine which of these caused a specific failure.

— behaviors that appear only when multiple components interact in specific ways that none would produce in isolation. These failures are invisible to component-level monitoring. Only end-to-end trace correlation exposes them.
— trace a user-facing agent failure to the specific LLM call, tool invocation, or agent decision that caused it, reducing mean time to resolution from hours to minutes.
— token usage and inference cost per agent task, per task type, and per agent component, enabling data-driven optimization decisions.
— when a new model or prompt version is deployed, unified metrics immediately show whether task success rate, latency, or token efficiency changed.
— correlate token throughput with infrastructure utilization to predict when serving capacity needs to increase before agents start queuing.
— structured logging of LLM inputs and outputs at each step allows post-hoc analysis of why the model produced a specific incorrect response.
— in regulated industries, traces provide tamper-evident records of every decision an agent made and every data source it consulted.
— a high-frequency agent system can generate millions of spans per day. Storing full traces with all LLM inputs and outputs is expensive; sampling strategies must balance coverage with cost.
— agent inputs often contain PII or confidential documents. Logging LLM inputs verbatim into a trace store requires careful access control and potential redaction before storage.

— agents don't always follow the same path. Threshold-based alerts on step count or tool calls generate high false-positive rates. Quality-based alerting (task success rate, semantic similarity of output) is harder to implement.
— adding OTel tracing to every LLM call and tool invocation adds a few milliseconds per call. In tight agent loops, this overhead accumulates.
— teams using LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, and custom agents have different built-in tracing hooks, making a single unified pipeline harder to establish.
The instrumentation layer emits three types of signals as the agent executes.

form the trace tree: a root span covers the entire agent task; child spans cover each LLM call, each tool invocation, and each sub-agent. Spans carry attributes that describe the operation — model name, token counts, tool name, input/output truncated payloads, success/failure status.
are numerical time-series measurements: token throughput, request latency percentiles, error rates, queue depth, GPU utilization.
are structured event records: agent state changes, tool outputs, error messages, and reasoning checkpoints.

The OpenTelemetry (OTel) SDK provides a vendor-neutral instrumentation layer. Agent code calls OTel APIs to create spans and record attributes; the OTel SDK batches and forwards telemetry to an OTel Collector process. The Collector performs processing (sampling, attribute enrichment, sensitive field redaction) and exports to backend stores — Grafana Tempo for traces, Prometheus/Mimir for metrics, Loki or Elasticsearch for logs.
The power of unified observability comes from correlation. Every span, metric data point, and log event carries a
. When an engineer sees an anomaly in the token-usage metric dashboard, they can click a data point, get the

from the metric exemplar, jump directly to the distributed trace in Tempo showing every agent step, then pivot from any span to its structured log events. This three-way linkage — metrics → traces → logs — is the core value of a unified stack over three separate siloed tools.

The unified observability pipeline flows from agent-side instrumentation through collection, processing, and into queryable backends, with a visualization layer sitting on top of all three signal types.

attrs.alt: Unified observability pipeline diagram: Agent System with OTel SDK sending to OTel Collector, which exports to Traces, Metrics, Logs backends and a Grafana visualization layer

content.text: Standard OTel span attributes cover HTTP status and duration. Agent observability requires additional semantic attributes to make traces queryable by agent-specific dimensions:
— correlates all spans belonging to a single agent task execution.
— position of this span in the agent's reasoning sequence.
— for cost attribution and efficiency tracking.
— success/failure/partial, set on the root span when the task completes.

Unified observability is not optional for production agent systems — it is a prerequisite. You cannot operate agents reliably at scale without the ability to diagnose failures and track quality trends. The question is not whether to implement it, but at what level of investment for your current stage:
— structured logs with trace IDs and a basic Grafana dashboard over Loki is sufficient. Tools like LangSmith provide out-of-the-box tracing for LangChain agents with minimal setup.
— full OTel instrumentation with 100% trace sampling, Tempo for traces, Prometheus for metrics, and Grafana dashboards. Start here when agents serve real users.
— tail-based sampling (keep 100% of failed traces, sample 5% of successful ones), distributed trace storage with Tempo in object storage, and custom alerting on task success rate and p99 latency.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource

# Configure the tracer provider with service identity
resource = Resource.create({"service.name": "research-agent", "service.version": "1.2.0"})
provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
provider.add_span_processor(
    BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint="http://otel-collector:4317"))
)
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent.tracer")

async def run_agent_task(task_id: str, user_query: str) -> str:
    """Execute an agent task with full OTel instrumentation."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.task") as task_span:
        task_span.set_attribute("agent.task_id", task_id)
        task_span.set_attribute("agent.query_length", len(user_query))
        task_span.set_attribute("agent.task_type", "research")

        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_query}]
        total_input_tokens = 0
        total_output_tokens = 0

        for step in range(10):
            with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.llm_call") as llm_span:
                llm_span.set_attribute("agent.step_number", step)
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.model", "gpt-4o")
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.input_message_count", len(messages))

                response = await call_llm(messages)

                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.input_tokens", response.usage.prompt_tokens)
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.output_tokens", response.usage.completion_tokens)
                total_input_tokens += response.usage.prompt_tokens
                total_output_tokens += response.usage.completion_tokens

            content = response.choices[0].message.content
            if "DONE" in content:
                task_span.set_attribute("agent.task_outcome", "success")
                task_span.set_attribute("agent.steps_taken", step + 1)
                break
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": content})

        task_span.set_attribute("agent.total_input_tokens", total_input_tokens)
        task_span.set_attribute("agent.total_output_tokens", total_output_tokens)
        return content

import time
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import StatusCode

tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent.tools")

def traced_tool(tool_name: str):
    """Decorator that wraps any tool function with OTel instrumentation."""
    def decorator(fn):
        async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            with tracer.start_as_current_span(f"tool.{tool_name}") as span:
                span.set_attribute("tool.name", tool_name)
                span.set_attribute("tool.input_size", len(str(kwargs)))
                start = time.monotonic()
                try:
                    result = await fn(*args, **kwargs)
                    span.set_attribute("tool.success", True)
                    span.set_attribute("tool.output_size", len(str(result)))
                    span.set_status(StatusCode.OK)
                    return result
                except Exception as e:
                    span.set_attribute("tool.success", False)
                    span.set_attribute("tool.error_type", type(e).__name__)
                    span.record_exception(e)
                    span.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(e))
                    raise
                finally:
                    duration_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
                    span.set_attribute("tool.duration_ms", duration_ms)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

@traced_tool("web_search")
async def search_web(query: str, num_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    # ... actual search implementation
    pass

@traced_tool("code_executor")
async def execute_code(code: str, language: str = "python") -> dict:
    # ... actual execution implementation
    pass

from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.prometheus import PrometheusMetricReader
from prometheus_client import start_http_server

# Start Prometheus scrape endpoint on port 8888
start_http_server(port=8888)
reader = PrometheusMetricReader()
provider = MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader])
metrics.set_meter_provider(provider)
meter = metrics.get_meter("agent.metrics")

# Define agent-specific metrics
task_success_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_task_success_total",
    description="Number of agent tasks completed successfully",
)
task_failure_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_task_failure_total",
    description="Number of agent tasks that failed",
)
task_latency_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent_task_duration_seconds",
    description="End-to-end agent task duration in seconds",
    unit="s",
)
token_usage_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_tokens_used_total",
    description="Total tokens consumed by agent tasks",
)

# Use in your agent
def record_task_completion(task_type: str, outcome: str, duration_s: float, tokens: int):
    labels = {"task_type": task_type}
    if outcome == "success":
        task_success_counter.add(1, labels)
    else:
        task_failure_counter.add(1, labels)
    task_latency_histogram.record(duration_s, labels)
    token_usage_counter.add(tokens, labels)
Structured logging connects individual agent events to the trace context, enabling log queries to return results scoped to a specific task execution:

import structlog
from opentelemetry import trace

# Configure structlog to include OTel trace context
def add_trace_context(logger, method, event_dict):
    """Inject current span's trace_id and span_id into every log event."""
    current_span = trace.get_current_span()
    if current_span.is_recording():
        ctx = current_span.get_span_context()
        event_dict["trace_id"] = format(ctx.trace_id, "032x")
        event_dict["span_id"] = format(ctx.span_id, "016x")
    return event_dict

log = structlog.wrap_logger(
    structlog.get_logger(),
    processors=[add_trace_context, structlog.processors.JSONRenderer()],
)

async def tool_web_search(query: str) -> list:
    log.info("tool_invoked", tool="web_search", query_length=len(query))
    results = await _do_search(query)
    log.info("tool_completed", tool="web_search", result_count=len(results))
    return results

A customer support agent at a SaaS company handles 8,000 tickets per day across 12 agent instances. Three weeks after deployment, the support team reports that agents started giving incorrect refund policy answers. The engineering team opens the task success rate dashboard: it shows a 14% drop in the "refund" task category starting exactly 4 days ago.
They filter the trace list to failed refund tasks and open one trace. The trace shows 5 LLM spans and 2 tool spans. The second tool call —
(suspiciously small). The log event for that span shows the query
returning zero results. Cross-referencing the deployment log in the metrics dashboard reveals that the knowledge base was re-indexed 4 days ago with a new document schema that renamed the
field, breaking the agent's search query. Root cause found and fixed in under 30 minutes — what would have taken days without unified observability.
Logging raw LLM inputs without redaction
— agent prompts often contain user PII, API keys passed as context, or confidential documents. Always run a redaction pass before writing LLM inputs to a log store.
— 100% trace sampling for a high-volume agent is expensive. Implement tail-based sampling that always captures failed traces and a configurable sample rate for successful ones.
— instrumenting individual spans without a root span carrying the task outcome makes it impossible to correlate span-level data with user-visible quality.
— GPU utilization and API latency can be normal while agent quality has degraded. Add quality-layer alerts: task success rate, tool error rate, and token budget overruns.
— having traces in one tool, metrics in another, and logs in a third breaks the cross-signal correlation workflow. The value of unified observability requires the signals to be linkable.
Create a root span per agent task
— the root span is the unit of analysis for quality tracking. It should carry the task type, outcome, total token cost, and step count as attributes.
Propagate trace context across async boundaries
— when agents spawn sub-agents or fire async tool calls, explicitly propagate the trace context so child operations appear under the correct parent span.
— a Prometheus exemplar attaches the current
to a metric data point, enabling one-click navigation from a metrics spike to the traces that caused it.
Use structured logs, not string logs
. Structured logs are queryable; string logs require regex parsing.
Define SLOs for agent quality, not just infrastructure
— "99% of tasks complete successfully" and "P95 task duration under 30 seconds" are agent SLOs that matter to users. Alert on these, not just on HTTP 500 rates.

The OTel Collector is the component that scales when agent traffic grows. A single Collector handles roughly 10,000 spans per second on a 2-core container. For larger deployments, run a fleet of Collectors behind a load balancer with consistent hashing to colocate spans from the same trace on the same Collector instance (reducing the overhead of assembling traces from distributed spans).

Grafana Tempo scales trace storage cheaply by writing trace data directly to object storage (S3, GCS) and maintaining a small index for query routing. At 10 million traces per day (100 spans each), expect roughly 1-2 TB of object storage per month, costing under $30 at cloud rates — far cheaper than time-series databases for trace data.
— the CNCF standard for vendor-neutral instrumentation. Python, JS, Java, Go SDKs. The correct base for all new agent instrumentation — avoid proprietary SDKs that lock you to a single vendor.

— the leading open-source unified observability stack. Logs (Loki), traces (Tempo), and metrics (Mimir/Prometheus) are designed to inter-link via Grafana's Explore view. Best for teams that want full control over their observability stack.
— commercial observability platform with native LLM observability features (LLM Observability product) and OTel ingestion. Lowest setup friction for teams already using Datadog.
— purpose-built for high-cardinality distributed traces. Excellent for debugging complex agent behaviors where you need to query across many span attributes simultaneously.
— LangChain's hosted tracing and evaluation platform. Zero-config for LangChain agents. Best for teams using the LangChain ecosystem who want LLM-native debugging tools.
— open-source LLM observability with native support for tracing agent frameworks, embedding drift detection, and evaluation pipelines. Self-hostable.
— W&B's lightweight tracing tool for LLM applications. Simple SDK, good for teams already using W&B for ML experiment tracking.

Observability data is sensitive by nature — it captures exactly what your agents are doing, what data they processed, and what decisions they made. Securing the observability pipeline is as important as securing the agents themselves:
Field-level redaction in the OTel Collector
processor to mask PII patterns (email addresses, credit card numbers, SSNs) in span attributes before they reach the trace store.

— engineers should be able to query trace structure (span names, durations, outcomes) without necessarily accessing raw LLM prompt content. Implement data tiers: anonymized traces for wide access, full traces for security team only.

— run at least two Collector instances in active-active mode. If the Collector goes down, agents may fail if they're configured to block on telemetry export. Use async, non-blocking export and dead-letter queues.
— in regulated environments (finance, healthcare), trace data may need to be retained for 7 years with tamper-evidence. Write an immutable copy of task traces to WORM storage at the Collector level.

Think of unified observability as a flight data recorder for your agents. The flight recorder captures everything: cockpit audio (logs), instrument readings every millisecond (metrics), and the complete sequence of control inputs and system states (traces). When an incident happens, investigators don't guess — they replay the exact sequence of events. A unified observability system does the same for agent tasks: it makes non-deterministic, multi-step, multi-component failures reproducible and diagnosable from a desk.
The key shift from microservice observability is that an agent trace is not a call graph — it is a

. The span tree doesn't just show which services were called in what order; it shows which decisions the agent made, based on what evidence, and what actions it took as a result. Designing spans that capture this reasoning structure — not just timing and status codes — is the central challenge of agent observability.
Unified observability integrates logs, metrics, and traces into a single correlated pipeline — the three-way linkage is what enables rapid root cause analysis across agent failures.
OpenTelemetry is the correct vendor-neutral foundation; build on it from day one to avoid lock-in and to enable switching backends as requirements evolve.
Agent-specific span attributes (task_id, step_number, llm.model, tool.success, task_outcome) are required to make traces queryable by agent-meaningful dimensions, not just service-level ones.
Implement tail-based sampling for cost control — always capture failed traces, sample successful ones at 5-20% depending on traffic volume.
Redact sensitive content in the OTel Collector before it reaches storage — never log raw LLM inputs containing user data without a redaction step.
Quality-layer SLOs (task success rate, p95 task duration) matter more to users than infrastructure SLOs — alert on the former first.
An agent trace is a reasoning trace, not just a call graph — design spans to capture the agent's decision context, not merely its execution timing.
